title: Why Browser Fingerprinting Is More Powerful Than Cookies
excerpt: Browser fingerprinting identifies users based on technical characteristics of their device and browser—without relying on cookies.
contentHtml:
When people think about online tracking, they usually think of cookies. And while cookies used to be the primary method websites used to remember users, they are no longer the main tool for identification.

Browser fingerprinting is a technique used to distinguish devices based on technical characteristics such as screen resolution, system fonts, hardware features, network details and browser settings. These signals are combined into a statistical profile that can uniquely or semi-uniquely identify a user.

How fingerprinting works

Modern browsers expose a wide range of information for legitimate purposes—for example, to adapt content to device capabilities. Fingerprinting takes advantage of this by collecting many small signals, including:

  • Timezone and locale settings
  • Screen size and pixel density
  • Supported audio and video formats
  • Installed fonts
  • WebGL renderer details
  • Device memory and CPU cores
  • Network connection type

Individually, these details seem harmless. But together they can create a fingerprint that is stable even when cookies are deleted.

Is browser fingerprinting unique?

A fingerprint is rarely guaranteed to be globally unique, but it is often distinctive enough for tracking across sessions. Uniqueness depends on how uncommon a specific combination of settings is. For example, a standard desktop PC with default settings may be similar to many others, while a system with custom hardware, rare fonts or unusual locale settings stands out.

Can you prevent fingerprinting?

Completely blocking fingerprinting is difficult because many signals come from essential browser APIs. However, you can reduce how identifiable your device is by:

  • Using browsers that implement anti-tracking protections
  • Keeping browser settings close to defaults
  • Avoiding rare or highly customized configurations
  • Using privacy-oriented add-ons cautiously (some can increase uniqueness)

Fingerprinting is unlikely to disappear, because websites rely on some of the same signals for compatibility and security checks. But understanding how it works is an important part of maintaining privacy online.
